metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via Grep/Read) that a code-review-aware path exists in `rebar criteria eval` before documenting that 'project code-review ids resolve and calibrate through the code-review arm'; if no such path exists, either scope the implementation or narrow the documented contract to what the code actually does.
evidence: src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py lines 144–155: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry; if args.criterion_id not in registry.by_id(repo_root): sys.stderr.write(f'Error: unknown criterion ...')`
src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:criterion_prompt_id(): `return f'plan-review-{criterion_id.replace(".", "-")}'` — always prefixes 'plan-review-', no code-review arm
No code-review gate-keyed eval path found in _llm_eval_commands.py, eval.py, or anywhere in the codebase
findings.finding: Approach aspect 3 claims 'project code-review ids resolve and calibrate through the code-review arm' of `criteria eval`, but the actual implementation (`src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` `_criteria_eval`, lines 144–155) validates criterion ids exclusively against `plan_review.registry.by_id()`, and `src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:criterion_prompt_id()` unconditionally prefixes all ids with `plan-review-`. There is no code-review arm: a project code-review criterion passed to `rebar criteria eval` would be rejected as 'unknown criterion' by the plan-review registry lookup.
scenarios: A project author adds a code-review criterion (e.g. `project.no-print`) and runs `rebar criteria eval project.no-print` — it fails with 'unknown criterion' because the validator queries plan_review.registry, not the code-review registry.
The documentation section being added would describe a contract ('project code-review ids resolve and calibrate through the code-review arm') that the current code does not honor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the five required verbatim marker tokens (`round_a`, `logical criterion id`, `effective routing`, `criteria eval`, `configuration error`) explicit in the Approach or Scope section so an implementer knows to embed them in the section body — or relax the AC markers to match the natural-language aspect names the plan already uses (e.g. `round a` / `logical id`).
evidence: Plan approach text: 'Round A/B fan-in + logical id'
AC check marker: `'round_a'` (underscore form, not 'Round A' or 'Round-A')
AC check marker: `'logical criterion id'` (three-word phrase; plan says 'logical id')
These tokens appear only inside the Python one-liner in the AC, never as explicit authoring requirements in the Approach or Scope sections
findings.finding: The AC verification script checks for the literal marker token `round_a` (underscore) within the new section body, but the plan's own description of that aspect uses the phrase 'Round A/B fan-in + logical id'. An implementer following the plan's natural-language aspect descriptions will naturally write 'Round A' or 'Round-A' (not `round_a`), producing a correctly-documented section that nonetheless fails the automated marker check. The plan never tells the author to embed these exact lowercase-underscore tokens verbatim — the markers are only visible in the AC shell command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first AC bullet (the rtk shell command) vs Approach section aspect 1 description
scenarios: Author writes a well-structured section using 'Round A/B' and 'logical id' headings — correct content, but the AC assertion fails on the missing `round_a` token
Author writes 'logical id' without 'criterion' between the words — fails the `'logical criterion id'` marker check silently
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note under the Approach section (or inline with each aspect description) listing the exact marker string each aspect must contain, e.g.: 'Aspect 1 must include the token `round_a` and the phrase `logical criterion id`.' Alternatively, align the AC markers to the plan's own phrasing: replace `round_a` with `round a` and `logical criterion id` with `logical id`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or implement) that `criteria eval` actually dispatches `project.*` code-review criterion ids through the code-review solver arm before documenting this as a contract — either confirm the code-path exists (and update this plan's evidence), or scope the code change into this story alongside the doc.
evidence: Plan: 'criteria eval resolution — project code-review ids resolve and calibrate through the code-review arm'
Plan: 'This is a purely mechanical documentation change (no new testable behavior)'
_llm_eval_commands.py:147: `if args.criterion_id not in registry.by_id(repo_root):` — checks plan_review registry only, not code_review registry
eval_solver.py:187-201: `_code_review_prompt_id` matches only `code-review-*` prefix, never `project.*`
eval_solver.py:271-273: `_criterion_id` routes to plan-review passes, not code-review overlay runner
eval_solver.py:321: `raise ValueError(f'no eval solver for prompt {prompt_id!r}')` — the fallthrough for an unrecognized id
findings.finding: The plan's Aspect 3 ('`criteria eval` resolution — project code-review ids resolve and calibrate through the code-review arm') references a dispatch/routing protocol that the existing `criteria eval` code does NOT implement for `project.*` code-review criteria: `_llm_eval_commands.py:147` gates on `plan_review.registry.by_id()`, which will not contain a `project.*` id activated only under the `code_review` gate key, causing it to be rejected or fall through to `ValueError: no eval solver for prompt`. The plan claims this is 'purely mechanical documentation' with 'no new testable behavior', yet the behavior it would document (project code-review ids resolve via the code-review arm) is absent from the running code — creating a false contract.
findings.location: Approach section, Aspect 3; Acceptance Criteria AC line 1 marker 'criteria eval'; src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py:147 and src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py:187–280
scenarios: A maintainer reads the new doc section and runs `rebar criteria eval project.mycodereviewcriterion`; the command rejects it as 'not in the effective registry' even though the criterion is activated and working in code review — the documented contract is false.

An author who added a project code-review criterion tries to calibrate it via `criteria eval`; the command fails with a misleading 'activate a project criterion in .rebar/criteria_routing.json first' error despite the criterion being activated.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) verify there is a code path this analysis missed and cite it in the plan, or (b) narrow Aspect 3's documented claim to 'project code-review ids resolve through the code-review overlay runner in the gate workflow' (which IS implemented) and distinguish that from the `criteria eval` calibration path, noting calibration of project code-review criteria is a separate, deferred capability.

findings.finding: The Approach section requires four substantive content areas (Round A/B fan-in, repository-effective routing, criteria eval path, configuration-error behavior), but the AC only verifies presence of five token strings — a one-sentence stub containing all five strings satisfies the AC without documenting any of the four content areas meaningfully. There is a gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (single AC) vs. Approach section bullet list
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC assertion to also check for one representative phrase from each of the four Approach bullets, e.g. add `'fan-in'` (Round A/B), `'repository-effective'` (routing), `'resolution'` or `'admission'` (criteria eval path), and `'prompt/eval spec is missing'` (config-error behavior) to the `all(x in s ...)` list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan body that Aspect 1 requires **two** distinct literal marker strings ('round_a' AND 'logical criterion id') in the section text, and update the aspect count to five (or re-label the markers) so the plan description matches the five-item marker list in the proving command.
evidence: Plan body: 'four end-to-end contract aspects delivered by this epic'
Plan body second AC: 'documents each aspect as its own labelled paragraph/subheading (fan-in, Pass-3 routing, eval resolution, configuration error)'
AC command: markers=['round_a','logical criterion id','effective routing','criteria eval','configuration error'] — five items
AC inline comment: 'exit 0 = all five aspect markers present'
findings.finding: The plan body consistently describes 'four end-to-end contract aspects' and says 'all four aspects', but the verification command checks five distinct literal markers (['round_a','logical criterion id','effective routing','criteria eval','configuration error']) and the AC description explicitly says 'five aspect markers'. An executor who structures the new section around four headings may naturally roll 'round_a' and 'logical criterion id' into a single paragraph without including both strings verbatim, causing the check to fail. The plan never states that aspect 1 requires two separate literal markers.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first AC bullet; plan body 'four end-to-end contract aspects' and 'all four aspects'
scenarios: An executor writes four clearly-labelled subsections, uses 'round-trip fan-in' and 'criterion identifier' phrasing instead of the exact strings 'round_a' and 'logical criterion id', and the proving command fails despite a substantively correct section.
findings.suggested_fix:
In the plan body, replace 'four end-to-end contract aspects' with 'five verifiable aspects' and split Aspect 1 into two entries:
1. **Round A/B fan-in** — … (must include the literal token `round_a`)
2. **Logical criterion id** — … (must include the literal token `logical criterion id`)
Then update the second AC bullet's parenthetical list from '(fan-in, Pass-3 routing, eval resolution, configuration error)' to '(round_a fan-in, logical criterion id, effective routing, criteria eval, configuration error)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Aspect 4 to identify which configuration-error phase(s) to document — activation-time (missing routing entry), load-time (missing prompt file), and/or calibration-time (missing eval spec) — so the section covers the complete contract and the AC marker is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan: 'Configuration-error behavior — an activated criterion with a missing prompt/eval spec raises a located error'
overlay.py:319: `raise CriteriaError(f'criteria overlay {_overlay_path(rr)}: activated criterion {aid!r} has no {gate_key!r} routing entry')` — activation phase
eval.py:82: `raise EvalError(f'no eval spec for prompt {prompt_id!r} at {path}: {exc}')` — calibration phase
Plan mentions both 'missing prompt' and 'missing eval spec' as triggering the error, but they produce different error types at different phases
findings.finding: The plan's Aspect 4 ('configuration-error behavior — an activated criterion with a missing prompt/eval spec raises a located error') references a single 'located error' behavior but the runtime actually has THREE distinct error paths at different phases: (1) activation-time `CriteriaError` when the routing entry is missing (`criteria/overlay.py:319`), (2) fan-in/load-time `PromptNotFoundError` when the prompt file is absent, and (3) calibration-time `EvalError` when the eval spec file is missing (`eval.py:82`). The plan does not specify which of these phases/errors constitutes the 'configuration error' the documentation should cover, nor whether the section should document all three, leaving the documentation author no decision rule to follow.
findings.location: Approach section, Aspect 4; Acceptance Criteria AC line 1 marker 'configuration error'; src/rebar/llm/criteria/overlay.py:319, src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:82
scenarios: The documentation author writes about only one of the three error paths (e.g., only the activation-time error), omitting the fan-in PromptNotFoundError — leaving the calibration-path error undocumented and inconsistent with what a user sees when they forget to add the eval spec.
A reader of the doc section tests the 'located error' contract by omitting the prompt file and finds the error message format differs from what 'located error' implies.

findings.finding: The plan's Context section asserts that `docs/llm-framework.md` 'says projects may add code-review criteria through the same overlay as plan-review', but a full read of `docs/llm-framework.md` finds no such statement — the file contains zero mentions of 'project', 'overlay', 'criteria', or 'plan-review' in the body text. The premise that llm-framework.md already surfaces the project-criteria overlay capability for code review is false.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — 'docs/llm-framework.md documents the four-pass contract and says projects may add code-review criteria through the same overlay as plan-review'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'says projects may add code-review criteria through the same overlay as plan-review' with 'does not document how project code-review criteria flow through the pipeline, nor the overlay mechanism that enables them'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Context section: `docs/llm-framework.md` does NOT currently mention code-review project criteria or the overlay; the entire 'Code-review project criteria' section is net-new documentation, not a supplement to existing text.
evidence: Plan text: 'docs/llm-framework.md documents the four-pass contract and says projects may add code-review criteria through the same overlay as plan-review'
Full read of docs/llm-framework.md (485 lines) finds zero occurrences of 'criteria', 'overlay', 'project.', or any mention of project-supplied criteria
The search query 'criteria' against docs/llm-framework.md returned no matches
findings.finding: The Context section asserts that `docs/llm-framework.md` currently 'says projects may add code-review criteria through the same overlay as plan-review', but a full read of `docs/llm-framework.md` shows this claim is false — the file contains no mention of criteria, overlays, or project-supplied criteria of any kind. The premise is inverted: the doc currently says nothing of the sort, which is the stated motivation for this ticket.
scenarios: A plan reviewer treating this as evidence that the contract is already partially documented in llm-framework.md would be misled — no such text exists and the whole section is net-new.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'says projects may add code-review criteria through the same overlay as plan-review, but it does not document the runtime contract' with 'does not document how a project code-review criterion flows through the pipeline — this section is entirely absent'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Give AC2 a distinct verification step (e.g., a regex asserting `##` or bold-label prefixes before each marker) so it independently checks structure beyond marker presence.
evidence: AC1: 'verified by a content check asserting the presence of a distinct marker for each aspect... run rtk .venv/bin/python -c ...'
AC2: 'verified by the same command finding each marker within the section body (not merely once in the whole file)'
Both ACs reference 'the same command' — the same Python one-liner — so they have identical pass/fail outcomes.
findings.finding: AC1 and AC2 share identical verification (literally 'verified by the same command'), making AC2 redundant as a distinct acceptance criterion — passing AC1's shell command automatically satisfies AC2, so the second criterion adds no independent signal.
scenarios: An implementer adds a single paragraph mentioning all five markers without distinct subheadings; AC1's command passes; AC2 also passes (same command) even though the structural intent of AC2 (labelled subheadings) is unmet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC1 prose with the command by changing 'all four aspects' to 'all aspects (five required markers)' or by consolidating the two aspect-1 markers into one to match the prose count.
evidence: AC1 prose: 'documents all four aspects'
AC1 command: "markers=['round_a','logical criterion id','effective routing','criteria eval','configuration error']" — five strings
Approach section: four numbered aspects, with aspect 1 bundling two sub-concepts (Round A/B fan-in + logical id)
findings.finding: AC1 prose says 'documents all four aspects' but the verification command checks five markers (['round_a','logical criterion id','effective routing','criteria eval','configuration error']), creating a minor count mismatch that could confuse an implementer.
scenarios: An implementer reads 'four aspects' and writes four paragraphs, one covering fan-in alone (no 'logical criterion id' marker), and the command fails unexpectedly.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'documents all four aspects, verified by a content check asserting the presence of a distinct marker for each aspect' to 'documents all aspects, verified by a content check asserting the presence of five required markers (two for aspect 1)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
